round_summary:
Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL
P4 immediately pointed to P2 as the common thread across both failures, while P2 countered by citing the vote record and noting that P4 approved both failed teams yet blamed others each time. P3 deflected by questioning why P4 was allowed on Quest 1 despite earlier warnings, and P5 expressed moderate suspicion toward P3 for prior teaming suggestions but acknowledged uncertainty about P2. The round ended with P2 and P4 in direct conflict over blame attribution, and P3 under growing scrutiny from P5 for earlier positioning behavior.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P4 blamed P1 for both failing the first quest and voting against the second team, while P3 cited the overlapping quest memberships to argue P4 and P5 were the pair. P5 countered that one of the approving players (P2 or P3) must have sabotaged, and P2 explained voting yes was rational to avoid the Quest 1 suspects but now leaned toward P5 and P4 as the pair. Suspicion spread across all players with no clear consensus, leaving P1 isolated by voting behavior, P5 newly suspect for being on the failed quest, and P3's overlap logic pointing at P4 and P5.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P5 defended the team selection as choosing straightforward players after Quest 1, while P2 cited the overlap between failed quests to argue suspicion pointed at P4 or P5 rather than P2 or P3, and noted P1's lone no vote matched their suspicion of P5. P4 justified voting yes by claiming the Quest 1 composition made blame inevitable regardless of actions, while P3 applied logic to argue the fail indicated evil among the three-person team but likely not both P2 and P3, pointing suspicion primarily at P5 and secondarily at P4. The round solidified P2 and P3's alliance in defending each other while isolating P4 and P5 as the primary suspects, with P1's voting pattern reinforcing distrust of P5's leadership.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P4 pointed to P1's lone no-vote as suspicious distancing from a team P4 claimed P1 knew would fail, while P2 cited the overlap between failed quests to argue P1 remained the common thread. P5 explained placing P3 on the team to test P3's confidence from the previous round, and P3 defended the yes-vote by suggesting the failure likely came from players who had been excused rather than from P3 himself. Tension now centers on P1, who voted against both failed quests, versus P3, who served on the second failure and deflected blame outward.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P3 blamed P2 for pushing a convenient narrative and pointed to P1's no vote as a warning the group ignored. P2 argued that the overlap between failed quests points to P3 and P4 as suspects, while P5 defended the decision to include herself as a known variable but acknowledged the outcome looked bad. P4 protested being blamed for Quest 1's failure and suggested P5's choice to include P3 was risky, leaving tension focused on P3, P4, and suspicion beginning to shift toward P5's leadership judgment.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P2, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P3 highlighted that P4 appeared on both failed quests and suggested a pattern of blame-shifting, while P5 cited the voting record to argue P4 was the simplest common factor and the likeliest saboteur. P4 defended the team selection by explaining P2 and P5 seemed most trustworthy after Round 1, and P2 expressed confusion since P2 had advocated for this exact trio but now doubted whether removing P1 and P3 was the right call. Suspicion now centers heavily on P4, with P3 attempting to redirect scrutiny and P2 caught between trusting P4's rationale and questioning P2's own judgment.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P2, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P3 highlighted that P4 appeared on both failed quests and praised P1 for being the sole dissenter, while P2 pointed to P3 and P4 as the overlapping suspects across both missions. P4 challenged P2's logic by questioning why P2 approved the team if it seemed unsafe, and P5 expressed regret for supporting the proposal, calling out both P4 and P3 as no longer trustworthy. The round solidified P4 as the primary suspect, with P3 and P2 emerging as secondary points of contention heading into the next leader's decision.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P2, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P3 deflected blame from the leaders onto P2 for greenlighting P4's inclusion, while P2 cited the quest records to argue at least one of P4 or P5 must be responsible since P2 claimed to be good. P4 pushed to exclude P3 from future teams and proposed P1-P2-P5 for the next round, while P5 over-explained why P5 had approved the team and insisted P4 was more suspicious. Trust in P4 eroded sharply, with P2 and P5 both pointing at P4 as the likely saboteur, though P3's aggressive deflection onto P2 introduced new tension that may carry forward.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P2, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P3 argued P1 was the common factor across both failed missions and questioned why P4 would sabotage their own team, while P2 cited the voting record to narrow suspicion to P4 or P5 and accused P4 of framing by pushing to drop both P1 and P3 last round. P4 defended the team rotation as necessary and redirected focus to P1's blocking vote, prompting P5 to lean toward P4 as the likely culprit and propose sitting out either P3 or P4 next round to isolate the source of the failures. Tension between P2 and P4 solidified, with P5's proposal to rotate out suspects setting the stage for next round's team composition debate.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P2, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P4 immediately deflected blame onto P1 for the lone rejection vote, while P5 cited the mission overlap to suspect P3 and P4 as the probable pair. P3 turned suspicion back onto P1 by highlighting P1's presence on the first failed mission and sole NO vote on the second, framing P1 as steering outcomes. P2 suggested removing both P3 and P4 from future teams to test the hypothesis, marking those two as the emerging targets while P1's cautious vote became a contested signal of either prudence or sabotage.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P2 deflected blame from the first failure onto P3 for selecting P4 without hesitation, while P5 cited the quest outcomes to argue that at least one person from the first team and one from the second must be responsible. P3 defended the team composition as an intentional fresh start and insisted P3 played PASS, while P4 turned suspicion back onto P1 for blocking the vote and framed P1 as the consistent problem across both rounds. The dispute now centers on whether P4's inclusion or P1's dissent is the key warning signal going forward.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (only P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P2 immediately blamed P1 for steering the failures despite P1 not being on the quest, while P5 pointed to the voting and quest records to argue that both P2 and P4 must be suspect since each was on one failed mission. P3 explained that P3 had included P4 to test P4's earlier vocal opposition to P2, and after the fail declared P3 would not take P4 again, while P4 insisted P2 was the consistent link and accused P2 of always deflecting blame. The exchange left P3 and P4's alliance fractured and P2 under sustained pressure from P4 and P5, with P1's no-vote still drawing suspicion from P2.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL
P3 laid out the vote and quest history, noting P1's sole rejection of the team, while P5 reasoned that P2 or P4 must be responsible since they span both failed quests and P1 was absent from the second. P2 suggested the group was overreacting to early failures, while P4 redirected focus back to P2's presence on the first mission and push for rotation. Tension now centers on P2 versus P4 as the likely saboteur, with P1's dissenting vote and P5's logic shaping the debate going forward.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (only P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P2 immediately redirected attention away from the first quest failure and pointed to P3's leadership, while P5 and P3 both cited P4's presence on the failed mission as grounds for suspicion, with P5 suggesting P2 and P4 might both be involved. P4 pushed for splitting quest membership to isolate the saboteur, framing it as practical rather than defensive. P3's public suspicion of P4 and P5's direct accusation set up P4 as the primary target heading into Round 3, while P2's deflection and P1's lone dissent remained unresolved tensions.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P2 argued the pattern of failures across different teams pointed to voters manipulating team approval rather than mission members, while P5 cited the vote record to establish that at least one player from each failed quest must be untrustworthy and noted P1's lone rejection despite not being on the team. P4 accused P3 of recklessness or cover by proposing untested players, and P3 countered that the failure proved one of P4 or P5 sabotaged and urged future leaders to exclude P1. Tension now centers on whether P3's team selection was naïve or calculated, with P2's voter-focused argument creating doubt about those who approved both failed quests, while P5's logic chains the failures directly to mission participants.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P2 defended the team composition as unlikely to be an intentional setup, while P4 pointed to P2 as the common factor across both failed quests and argued the overlap was the simplest explanation. P5 pushed back against focusing on P2, redirecting attention to P1's presence on the first fail and P3's consistent yes votes, while P3 estimated a 70% probability P2 was involved and proposed rotating to a P1-P3-P4 team. The discussion left P2 under the most suspicion due to the overlap logic, though P5's defense of P2 and redirection toward P1 created a secondary thread of doubt that will likely surface in the next round.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Approved (only P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P2 acknowledged being the only overlap between both failed quests and suggested this made P2 the obvious suspect, while P4 and P3 both cited the voting and quest records to argue P2 was indeed most likely responsible. P5 challenged this consensus by pointing out that P3 and P4 had approved the team themselves and argued P1 remained the original source of failure from Quest 1. The round solidified P2 as the primary suspect in P3 and P4's eyes, while P5's defense of P2 drew quiet scrutiny and P1's dissenting vote went largely undiscussed.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P5 quickly blamed P3 for the second failure while P4 countered by highlighting that P2 was the only player on both failed quests, drawing group attention to the overlap. P2 acknowledged the overlap but argued that two different saboteurs could be responsible and suggested testing untested combinations like P1 with P3 and P5, while P3 put 65–70% suspicion on P2 yet remained open to being benched to reduce risk. The emerging tension centers on P2's presence in both failures versus P5's insistence on looking elsewhere, with P4's evidence-based argument likely to anchor future team proposals around avoiding P2 or isolating untested players.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P2 deflected blame away from the leader role and questioned why P4 switched from rejecting to joining the team, while P3 cited both quest results to identify P2 as the common thread between failures. P4 explained the approval by noting P1's removal but concluded P2 was the simplest explanation, also flagging P5's pattern of approving failed teams, while P5 suggested the group already agreed P2 wasn't the issue and proposed rotating to P3, P4, and one untested player. The round established P2 as the primary suspect due to overlap evidence, creating tension between P2's self-defense and the mounting logical case against P2, with P5's deflection attempt largely ignored.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P2 defended the leadership by questioning why P1's dissenting vote mattered if the quest failed anyway, while P3 and P4 both cited P2's presence on both failed quests as the simplest explanation for sabotage. P5 argued that overlap alone wasn't proof and redirected attention to P1's vote record as strategically suspect. The round solidified P2 as the primary suspect for most players, though P5's effort to shift blame onto P1 introduced friction that will likely carry into the next proposal.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: PASS

P4 argued that P1 was the common factor between the failed quest and suspicious voting patterns, attempting to shift blame away from the Round 1 failure, while P2 justified the test team and countered that P4's immediate blame of P1 looked like deflection. P5 cited the successful mission as proof of innocence and questioned why P1 voted against a reasonable team, while P3 assessed that at least one player from the failed Round 1 team (P1 or P4) was likely responsible, leaning toward P4 but keeping P5 as a smaller concern. The round solidified tension between P4 and P1, with P2 and P3 increasingly skeptical of P4's defensive positioning.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: FAIL

P4 pressed the case that P1's rejection of the second team and presence on the first failure made P1 the common problem, while P2 cited both failed quests to argue P5 appeared most suspect if P1 were trustworthy. P5 countered by noting P5 wasn't on the first mission and suggested P1's No vote undermined good candidates, and P3 proposed excluding anyone on two failures—currently only P5—shifting the group toward isolating P5 as the next round's likely exclusion target.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P1, P2, P4 | Vote: Unanimous approval | Outcome: FAIL

P3 pointed to the vote record showing the only difference between the passing first quest and this failed quest was P4's presence, building a clear case for suspicion. P4 defended by noting the entire group had approved the team unanimously, while P5 argued that P4's earlier request to join a team was normal behavior and shouldn't trigger automatic blame. P2 acknowledged the evidence looked bad for P4 but also validated P4's defensive response, leaving P4 under primary suspicion heading into the next round while P5's defense of P4 created a notable alignment between them.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved (only P1 voted no) | Outcome: PASS

P4 cited the successful quest as evidence clearing P5 and redirected focus back to the Round 1 failure involving P1 and P4, while P2 justified the team composition by arguing the pass showed at most one member could be suspect and proposed avoiding the P1/P4 pairing going forward. P5 dismissed the Round 1 vote record as uninformative and reinforced P4's framing that P1 remained the primary suspect, while P3 expressed lingering doubt about both P1 and P4 but acknowledged the possibility of strategic cooperation. The discussion solidified a tentative alliance around P2, P3, and P5, with P1 increasingly isolated and P4 successfully positioning P1 as the central threat despite sharing the failed mission.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved (only P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P4 immediately questioned why P1 voted against the team yet it still failed, implying P1 might be responsible, while P2 cited the lack of overlap between the two failed quests to argue that both P4 and P5 remain uncleared. P5 defended the yes-vote by calling the team reasonable and denying any motive to sabotage, and P3 offered probabilistic reasoning but noted P4's eagerness to blame P1 felt forced. Tension now centers on P4's repeated accusations toward P1, P5's defensive posture after appearing on the failed mission, and P2's methodical evidence-sharing that kept suspicion distributed across both prior teams.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P2, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P3 deflected blame onto P2 for pushing rotation that led to the failed quest, while P4 rationalized the failure by explaining the team followed the table's request for cleaner information and questioned why suspicion automatically fell on P4. P2 cited the voting record to argue that P4 must be responsible since P1 was the common denominator across both quests, and demanded P4 be excluded from Round 3, while P5 weighed P4 as more likely culpable but hedged by acknowledging P2 wasn't entirely cleared either. The round ended with a clear divide: P2 and P5 called for P4's exclusion and a P2+P5 core, while P3 continued to shield P4 and shift focus toward P2, setting up a tense leader decision for Round 3.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P1 | Team: P1, P2, P3 | Vote: Approved (P4 voted no) | Outcome: PASS

P4 downplayed the significance of the passing quest by arguing it was low-variance and cautioned against trusting the yes-voters too much, while P5 framed the result as confirmation that P1 and the team were reliable and suggested P4's no-vote was unusual. P2 defended the clean outcome as meaningful evidence and questioned what alternative P4 would have preferred, and P3 directly challenged P4's attempt to complicate what seemed like a straightforward success. The round solidified trust in P1, P2, and P3 among most players, while P4's skeptical stance and lone no-vote became a new focal point of suspicion heading into the next round.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P2 framed the pass as clearing the team and suggested the Round 1 fail was likely P1's doing, while P4 cautioned that the pass doesn't eliminate all suspicion since passing early can be a strategic choice. P3 redirected focus back onto the Round 1 pair (P1 and P2), arguing the data points more toward them, and P5 expressed uncertainty about whether P2 failed Quest 1 and is now rebuilding trust or whether P1 was responsible. The group remains divided on whether to trust P2 or continue testing outside the original duo, setting up tension over team composition in the next round.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P1, P2, P5 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P4 immediately blamed P2 for the failure, arguing the quest failed the moment P2 joined the team, while P2 countered with the voting record to point suspicion at P5, noting P1 had passed Quest 1 but this team failed. P5 deflected by questioning why P4 received no scrutiny despite being on the successful first quest, and P3 expressed 70/30 confidence that P5 caused the failure but acknowledged uncertainty since the first quest could have been passed deliberately for cover. The conflict between P2 and P5 intensified sharply, with P4 avoiding direct blame and P3 advocating for a next-round team that excludes either P4 or P5 to test competing theories.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P1, P2, P3 | Vote: Approved 4–1 (P4 voted no) | Outcome: PASS

P4 preemptively justified the sole no vote by framing it as caution rather than obstruction and urged future leaders to trust the proven core trio. P2 emphasized the clean pass validated at least two of the three on the team and flagged P4's dissenting vote as worth monitoring in future rounds. P5 pushed to avoid overthinking the success and keep building from the core, while P3 expressed relief and openly questioned why P4 would reject a team that successfully passed. P4's isolated no vote became the focal tension, with P2 and P3 signaling they will scrutinize whether P4 continues resisting trusted players.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: PASS

P2 claimed the successful quest validated the team and dismissed Round 1 concerns as overblown, while P4 pointed out that P2 remains a suspect from the Round 1 failure and questioned why P1 rejected the team. P3 emphasized P2's presence on both missions as the only hard fact and suggested P4 could be trusted based on the clean pass, while P5 expressed lingering suspicion of P2 as the common thread but acknowledged reduced certainty. The debate left P2 and P3 under scrutiny from P4 and P5, with P1's dissenting vote unexplained and likely to fuel distrust in the next round.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P1, P3, P5 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P5 pointed to the common thread of failed quests including "sketchy slots" and argued the table greenlighted P3's team too easily, while P2 used the Quest 1 failure to lock P4 as culpable and narrowed the second suspect to P5 or P3 based on Quest 2's composition. P4 questioned whether the table was scapegoating and noted P3's choice to include P5 on a three-person team that still failed, prompting P3 to admit the pick was a mistake and commit to proposing P1, P2, and P3 next round. Trust in P4 eroded further while P3's self-critique and proposed tight trio suggested P5 would face mounting scrutiny moving forward.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P1 | Team: P1, P2, P3 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P4 argued the pass only proved P1 avoided obvious mistakes and insisted Quest 1's failure still pointed to P1 or P5, while P5 agreed the pass supported P2 and P3 but maintained the fail likely came from P1 or P5. P2 proposed keeping P2 and P3 together and testing one of P1 or P5 on the next mission to isolate the saboteur, and P3 corrected their earlier misunderstanding of the rules while leaning suspicion toward P4 and P5. The group now views P2 and P3 as likely trustworthy, with P1 and P5 remaining under scrutiny as the source of Quest 1's failure.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P1 | Team: P1, P2, P3 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P4 immediately cautioned that the pass doesn't clear P2 or P3 since passes can be used strategically, arguing the round revealed little, while P3 countered that a three-person pass is more informative than the prior two-person mission and proposed testing P4 or P5 individually next round. P5 urged maintaining the core team with minimal rotation, and P2 suggested P4 and P5 are more likely suspects than anyone in the passed trio. The discussion solidified P1/P2/P3 as a tentative trusted bloc while placing scrutiny on P4 and P5, with P3's testing proposal gaining traction and P4's skepticism creating mild tension.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P2, P4 | Vote: Unanimous approval | Outcome: FAIL

P5 and P3 both concluded the saboteur must be P2 or P4 since P1 was on the successful Quest 1 team with P3, and they pushed to exclude both P2 and P4 from the next proposal while keeping the P1/P3 core with P5. P2 argued that repeated overlap makes P1 less trustworthy as a baseline and questioned why the same players were being treated as safe, while P4 defended the team composition by claiming P1 was added for success and P2 to test someone new. The blame now centers sharply on P2 and P4, with P3 and P5 forming a clear consensus to sideline them both moving forward.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P2, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P2 deflected blame onto P4 for leading the failed mission, while P4 argued that the failure most likely pointed to P2 as the new addition since P1 had been on the passing Round 1 team. P5 reinforced this logic by noting the simplest explanation was P2's guilt, though P3 cautioned against over-confident reads based on a single failure. The primary tension now centers on P2, with P4 and P5 aligned in suspicion while P2 attempts to redirect scrutiny back toward the leader.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (P1 voted no) | Outcome: PASS

P2 argued the pass clears P2 and suggested P1 was responsible for Quest 1's failure, while P3 framed P1's no vote as suspicious and inconsistent with information-gathering. P4 welcomed the pass and advocated keeping two members from the successful team, while P5 remained cautious about P1 and P2 but expressed more confidence in P4 and P5. The round left P1 isolated as the sole dissenter, with P2 and P3 both pointing to P1's vote as a red flag, setting up P1 as the likely flashpoint for Round 3 debates.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P3 | Team: P3, P4, P5 | Vote: Approved (only P1 voted no) | Outcome: FAIL

P4 argued that P1's no vote was steering the table toward easy scapegoats, while P5 cited concrete evidence that at least one of P3 or P4 must have caused the fail since P5 claimed to be good. P2 questioned why P3 chose P4 over testing away from the Quest 1 fail pair, and P3 acknowledged the mistake but leaned 70/30 toward suspecting P4 over P5. Tension now centers on P4's role in both failed quests, with P1's dissenting vote becoming a secondary point of scrutiny and P3's team-building judgment under question.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P2, P3, P5 | Vote: Approved but P1 voted no | Outcome: PASS

P5 justified excluding P4 after Round 1's failure and expressed confidence in P2 and P3 after the successful quest, while P4 highlighted P1's lone rejection vote as suspicious and deflected earlier accusations. P2 defended the team composition by citing P4's earlier suggestions, and P3 proposed testing P1 or P4 individually on the next mission to isolate the source of Round 1's failure. The successful outcome shifted focus toward P1's dissenting vote and created pressure to test P1 and P4 separately rather than together.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P2, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P4 argued the quest outcome validated the team as trustworthy and suggested continuing with the same players, while P2 pushed hard for repeating the exact lineup claiming all three were cleared. P3 warned that passing Round 2 is a common trust-building tactic and cautioned against treating anyone except possibly P1 as fully confirmed, while P5 asserted that the pass proved no spies were present because they would have taken an easy sabotage. The tension centers on whether the successful quest genuinely clears P2 and P4 or whether skepticism should remain, with P3's caution likely to fuel debate over team composition in the next round.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P2, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P2 immediately deflected blame toward P1 as the common member across both quests, while P5 outlined the logical deduction that either P2 or P4 caused the failure and proposed isolating P2 and P3 in future teams. P4 defended the team composition as an information-gathering move and confidently accused P2, but P3 countered by suggesting P4's quick accusation was suspicious and advocated testing P4 by excluding him next round. The sharpest tension now sits between P4 and P2, with P3 amplifying doubt about P4's leadership and P5 anchoring the group to vote-record logic.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P1, P2, P5 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P5 emphasized the successful track record and discouraged paranoia, while P4 proposed testing P3 next since P3 remained untested across both missions. P3 claimed credit for maintaining momentum and volunteered for the next quest, while P2 offered vague agreement without strong opinions. The round reinforced trust in P1, P2, and P5 as the proven core, with growing pressure to include P3 in future missions to verify their trustworthiness.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P1, P2, P5 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P4 deflected blame onto P2 for riding a normal-looking team that failed, while P3 cited the quest history to argue that at least one of P2 or P5 must be responsible and called for P5's exclusion next round. P2 defended the team choice as a safe core with P1 and questioned why an enemy would accept blame so openly, while P5 dismissed the accusations as narrative-driven and urged the group to move forward. The failure hardened P3's suspicion of P5 and created tension between P4's blame of P2 and P2's self-defense, setting up a likely clash over whether to exclude P5 or scrutinize P2 in the next round.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P1 | Team: P1, P3, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P2 deflected blame from Quest 1 back onto P5 and highlighted P3's inclusion on the failed team, while P5 cited the zero-overlap between quests to argue one fail source must be in {P2, P5} and another in {P3, P4}. P3 shifted focus onto P4's willingness to approve the team despite the prior failure, framing hesitation as a good-player trait, while P4 defended the vote as information-gathering but acknowledged appearing suspicious and suggested excluding both P2 and P3 moving forward. Suspicion now centers on P2 versus P3 as the likeliest fail source, with P4 caught between both accusations and P5's logic-driven case gaining traction among those weighing the set-theory argument.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P2 | Team: P2, P3, P4 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P4 argued the pass doesn't clear P2 because the failed Quest 1 still implicates P1 or P2, while P2 and P3 pushed to interpret the pass as clearing the entire team and shift focus away from themselves. P5 remained cautious, suggesting P2 stay off the next team to test whether another pass occurs without P2, while P3 redirected attention to P1 as the loudest target. The round ended with P2 and P3 gaining tentative credibility from the pass, but P4 and P5 resisting a full clear and advocating rotation to isolate P2's influence.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P4 | Team: P1, P4, P5 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: PASS

P2 downplayed the significance of quest outcomes and redirected attention to P3's Round 1 proposal, while P3 countered by citing the voting record and quest results to argue P2 remained the likeliest source of the earlier failure. P4 explained the team composition as a deliberate move away from Round 1 suspects, and P5 encouraged the group to build future teams around the successful P1/P4 core. The exchange solidified P2 and P3 as the primary suspects heading into Round 3, with P1, P4, and P5 emerging as a tentative trusted bloc.

Round 2 Summary:
Leader: P5 | Team: P1, P2, P5 | Vote: Unanimous | Outcome: FAIL

P5 argued the failure proved one of the three team members must be blamed rather than leadership itself, while P2 cited the contrasting outcomes of rounds 1 and 2 to argue P5 was the likeliest saboteur since P1 appeared on both missions. P3 questioned P2's certainty and defended P5's team-building logic as consistent with P4's earlier rotation suggestion, prompting P4 to note that P3 and P5 seemed aligned and propose splitting them in future tests.
